text: here i'm showing a case of a left-sided indirect hernia a sliding sigmoid this is a young patient with a very symptomatic hernia and i selected him for a tap approach i'm just starting my flap the peritoneum was pretty thin decided to go pre-transverse salus Usually do medial dissection first. And I left this case mostly unedited again. I think it helps for people to give more constructive feedback as I continue to try to improve. I've gotten comfortable with the tap approach. These cases take me just under an hour usually. I think this one might have been about forty-five minutes.
The surgeon attempts to extract the indirect hernia sac while identifying and sweeping the cord structures, intermittently checking to ensure the dissection is not too close to the bowel wall.
The surgeon identifies the edge of the true hernia sac during the procedure.
The surgeon carefully dissects along the edge of the hernia sac, identifying the vas deferens. The cord structures are further parietalized, and the surgeon searches for a lipoma.
The surgeon completes the MPO dissection and proceeds to place a 16 by 12 cm anatomic left program mesh over the dissected area.
The surgeon observes the mesh sitting nicely in place and proceeds to close the peritoneal flap over the mesh.
The surgeon completes the dissection and uses an angiocath to evacuate gas from the flap, ensuring the mesh remains flat during deflation.
